segments.caption: The video opens with a title card reading 'THE ANCIENT REMEDY OF DIABETES REVEALED' and 'INTENSIVE DIETARY MANAGEMENT DR JASON FUNG' against a red curtain background. The scene transitions to a lecture setting where Dr. Jason Fung, a man wearing glasses and a light-colored button-up shirt, stands in front of a projection screen displaying a graph titled 'It is Hard to Treat T2DM Adequately... Because T2DM is a Progressive Disease' from the ADOPT study. He gestures with his hands as he speaks, emphasizing points about the progression of type 2 diabetes over time, as shown by the rising HbA1c levels in the graph. The audience is partially visible in the foreground, with some attendees’ heads in view. A text overlay at the bottom of the screen reads 'For more information www.kidneylifescience.ca'. As the segment progresses, the slide changes to a new diagram titled 'Diabetes is getting worse!' showing a progression of treatments over time: Metformin → Metformin + Glyburide → Insulin → More Insulin, with a large blue arrow labeled 'Over Time'. Dr. Fung continues to gesture and explain, maintaining eye contact with the audience and using hand movements to illustrate the increasing complexity of diabetes management. The room is well-lit, with bookshelves filled with binders visible to the right of the screen, contributing to the academic atmosphere.

segments.caption: Dr. Jason Fung stands in front of a Projection Screen delivering a lecture on diabetes, actively gesturing with both hands to emphasize his points. The slide behind him displays a table titled 'Diabetes' with columns for Disease, Cause, Treatment, Symptom, and Symptomatic Treatment. The 'Cause' and 'Treatment' for 'Diabetes Insulin Resistance' are marked with red-circled '????', highlighting the lack of known cause and treatment. Below, 'Infection' is listed with 'Bacteria' as the cause and 'Antibiotic' as the treatment, contrasting with the diabetes row. The 'Symptom' for diabetes is 'High Blood Sugars', and 'Symptomatic Treatment' includes 'Insulin' and 'OHA', with a green arrow pointing upward labeled 'Does not alter disease process' and 'Acetaminophen' noted below. To the right of the screen, Bookshelves filled with white binders contribute to the academic atmosphere. The lighting remains consistent, and the setting is a professional lecture environment. segments.caption: Dr. Jason Fung continues his lecture, standing in front of the Projection Screen which displays a diagram titled 'What causes Insulin Resistance?'. Dr. Fung continues to gesture emphatically, underscoring the absurdity of the current treatment approach.

segments.caption: Dr. Jason Fung continues his lecture, now standing in front of a projection screen displaying a slide titled 'Current Treatment Paradigm: Idiotic'. The slide features a graph labeled 'A.' showing a negative correlation between 'Total Insulin Dose (Units/Day)' and 'Glucose Disposal Rate (μmol/kg/min)', with a green arrow indicating 'Increasing insulin resistance!'. Below the graph, a cartoon depicts two figures, one holding a syringe, with the text 'You can’t treat a hyperinsulinemic state with more insulin!' prominently displayed. Dr. Fung, wearing a light-colored button-up shirt and glasses, gestures expressively with both hands as he speaks, emphasizing his critique of current diabetes treatment approaches. segments.caption: Dr. Jason Fung continues his lecture, now standing in front of a projection screen displaying a slide titled 'Treatment Considerations'. The slide lists three key points: 1) The key to treatment is reducing, not increasing insulin; 2) Diabetes is a dietary disease requiring dietary treatment; 3) Diabetes is a curable disease, not a chronic one. Below these points, additional text reads, 'A simple way to lower insulin is intermittent fasting. Remember that fasting should NOT cause vomiting or GI upset.' Dr. Fung, wearing a light-colored button-up shirt and glasses, gestures expressively with both hands as he speaks, emphasizing his points. His movements are dynamic, often bringing his hands together or spreading them apart to underscore his message. The audience members are partially visible in the foreground, with the tops of their heads indicating an engaged live audience. To the right of the screen, bookshelves filled with white binders remain visible, reinforcing the academic and professional setting. The lighting is consistent, focused on Dr. Fung and the screen, creating a clear and focused atmosphere for the lecture.

segments.caption: The video begins with Dr. Jason Fung standing in front of a projection screen, delivering a lecture on energy storage in the body. He is actively gesturing with his hands, emphasizing points as he speaks, and occasionally adjusts his glasses or touches his head. The Projection Screen displays a slide titled 'How It Works,' listing two ways the body stores energy: sugar (glycogen) and fat (unlimited stores), with a note about energy storage at mealtimes. A brief inset video in the upper right corner shows a hand pouring blue liquid from a can into a glass, likely serving as a visual metaphor for energy consumption, before disappearing. The audience members are partially visible in the foreground, with several heads indicating an engaged live audience, some turning slightly to follow the speaker’s movements. The Bookshelves to the right of the screen are filled with white binders, reinforcing the academic setting. As the segment progresses, the slide changes to 'Case 1- Richard,' detailing a 55-year-old white male with diabetes since 2002, complications including retinopathy, neuropathy, and nephropathy, and who started an IDM program in June 2013. Dr. Fung continues to gesture and speak, maintaining eye contact with the audience. The On-Screen Text Overlay at the bottom of the screen reads 'More information kidneylifescience.ca,' providing a resource for viewers. The lighting remains consistent, and the atmosphere is professional and educational throughout.

segments.caption: The video begins with a still image of a man and a woman standing in a kitchen, the man appearing overweight and the woman smiling beside him, suggesting a personal or family context. The scene then transitions to a lecture setting where Dr. Jason Fung, wearing a light-colored button-up shirt and glasses, stands in front of a projection screen, delivering a presentation. He gestures actively as he explains data shown on the screen, which includes a graph titled 'Richard: Weight' showing a steady decline over weeks. The graph updates to 'Richard: Waist Size' and then 'Richard: HbA1c', indicating improvements in health metrics. A photo of a man in a red sweater appears on screen, likely representing Richard before or after treatment. The presentation continues with a graph titled 'Richard: Insulin', showing a sharp decline in insulin usage over time, with Dr. Fung emphasizing the data with hand movements and occasionally touching his head or adjusting his glasses. The atmosphere is academic and informative, with the audience partially visible in the foreground, engaged in the lecture. Bookshelves filled with white binders remain visible to the right, reinforcing the professional setting. The video concludes with a bold text overlay stating 'Diabetes - Cured', highlighting the success of the intervention discussed.

segments.caption: Dr. Jason Fung continues his lecture, standing in front of the Projection Screen, which now displays a new case study titled 'Case 3 - AP'. segments.caption: Dr. Jason Fung continues his lecture in front of a projection screen, which initially displays a slide titled 'Top 6 Ways to Reduce Insulin' listing six strategies: Intermittent Fasting, reducing dietary refined carbohydrates, eating a high natural fat diet, fibre, vinegar, and spices/herbs. As he speaks, he gestures expressively with his hands, occasionally touching his head or adjusting his glasses, emphasizing key points. Midway through the segment, the slide updates to 'Top 5 Ways to Reduce Insulin,' removing the third item ('Eat a high fat diet (natural fats)') from the list, though the speaker continues to reference the original six. The background remains consistent with bookshelves filled with white binders to the right of the screen. The audience is partially visible in the foreground, with heads of attendees indicating engagement. The lighting is steady and professional, maintaining focus on the speaker and the screen. No inset video or additional images appear during this segment, and the on-screen text overlay 'More information kidneylifescience.ca' remains at the bottom of the screen throughout.

segments.caption: Dr. Jason Fung continues his lecture, standing in front of the Projection Screen which now displays the 'Hormonal Obesity Theory' diagram. He gestures actively with his hands, pointing to various elements on the slide, including 'Increased Insulin level,' 'Fasting,' 'Insulin Resistance,' and the contributing factors like 'Fattening Carbohydrates,' 'Fructose,' and 'High Protein.' He moves slightly from side to side, engaging with the content as he explains the connections between insulin, metabolic syndrome, and related health conditions such as obesity, diabetes, and fatty liver. The Bookshelves remain visible to the right, filled with white binders, reinforcing the academic setting. The On-Screen Text Overlay 'More information www.kidneylifescience.ca' appears at the bottom of the screen, providing a resource for viewers. Dr. Fung occasionally adjusts his glasses and uses hand movements to emphasize key points, maintaining a dynamic and informative delivery. The audience remains partially visible in the foreground, attentive to the presentation.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with Dr. Jason Fung standing in front of a Projection Screen displaying a detailed diagram titled 'Hormonal Obesity Theory.' He is actively gesturing with his hands, emphasizing points as he speaks, and occasionally adjusts his glasses or touches his head, indicating engagement with the material. The diagram illustrates how factors such as 'Fattening Carbohydrates,' 'Fructose,' 'High Protein,' 'Fibre,' 'Fat,' and 'Vinegar' influence 'Increased Insulin level,' leading to 'Insulin Resistance' and 'Metabolic Syndrome,' which in turn contribute to conditions like obesity, diabetes, and fatty liver. To the right of the screen, Bookshelves filled with white binders are visible, reinforcing the academic setting. An On-Screen Text Overlay at the bottom reads 'More information www.kidneylifescience.ca,' providing a resource for viewers. The audience is partially visible in the foreground, with several heads indicating an engaged live audience. As the segment progresses, the scene transitions to a dark red curtain background, and the text 'THANKS FOR WATCHING WWW.KIDNEYLIFESCIENCE.CA' appears in white, centered on the screen, marking the conclusion of the presentation. The atmosphere shifts from an informative lecture to a formal closing, with no further movement or interaction from Dr. Fung or the audience.
